posts.thesis: After this post, a marketer can lead a title with the buyer’s literal search phrase so the right people find it.
posts.firstComment:
Turn one buyer search into 5 titles that actually rank -> https://sellontube.com/tools/youtube-title-generator?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=brand-presence

Or do it in ChatGPT / Claude (free plan):

You're a B2B YouTube SEO strategist. Topic: [your video]. Buyer: [role].
1. 5 phrases this buyer would likely search (candidates to validate).
2. Turn each into a title: their exact phrase up front + a differentiator tail.
3. Flag buying-intent vs curiosity.
Table: Phrase | Title | Intent. Then the 2 to make first.
Validate the phrases in YouTube autocomplete before filming.
posts.postAngle: Titles written for humans miss the buyer’s search.
posts.linkedinPost:
Your best video is invisible. The title did it.

I've watched genuinely great videos die under clever titles, and it always comes down to one thing.

You wrote the title for a human skimming a feed. But buyers do not skim, they search. They type an exact phrase into YouTube, and a clever title that shares none of those words never shows up.

A great video sits under the title "Churn, Solved." Nobody searches that. Your buyer types "how to reduce SaaS churn." Same video, 2 titles, and only 1 ever gets found.

Steal this title formula:
[the exact phrase your buyer searches] + [your differentiator]

Example:
"how to reduce SaaS churn" + "without dropping your price"
= How to reduce SaaS churn without dropping your price

Boring? A little. But a boring title a buyer searches beats a clever one nobody types, every time.

Look at your best video's title. Would a buyer ever actually type it into search?
posts.xPost:
Your best video is invisible.
The title did it.

You wrote it for someone skimming a feed.
But buyers don't skim. They search.

A clever title nobody types = a video nobody finds.

Lead with the exact phrase they'd search.
Boring beats clever here.

posts.thesis: After this post, a founder can give every video one specific next step so viewers become leads.
posts.firstComment:
Want the CTAs written for you? Paste into ChatGPT or Claude (free):

You're a B2B demand-gen expert. My video teaches: [topic]. I sell: [offer]. Viewer: [role].
Write 3 calls-to-action that fit THIS video (never "visit our site"):
- one comment-bait ("comment X and I'll send the [resource]")
- one low-friction ask (15-min mapping / resource in description)
- one soft next-video nudge
Each 1-2 sentences, said while they're still watching. Rank by likely conversion.
posts.postAngle: Videos with no next step waste the trust they earn.
posts.linkedinPost:
Your best video probably has no idea what to do next. So neither does the viewer.

The best video I ever helped make lost most of its leads in the last 5 seconds. It taught brilliantly, then just ended.

They watch, they nod, they get their answer, and they leave. No next step, no way to raise their hand. You taught them for free and waved goodbye.

Here is why that is expensive. Wyzowl found 89% of people say a video's quality shapes whether they trust a brand. So a good video does the hardest job in sales, earning trust, then strands that trust with nowhere to go.

The fix costs 1 sentence. Give every video a single, specific next step:
Not "check out our website." Too vague.
A resource that matches the video: "I made a 1-page checklist for this. Comment 'checklist' and I'll send it."
Or a low-friction ask: "Want this mapped to your setup? 15 minutes, link in the description."

Worried it feels salesy? It is not a pitch. It is telling someone who just got value where to get more.

Picture your last video's final 10 seconds. What, exactly, do you ask the viewer to do?
posts.xPost:
Your best video has no idea what to do next.
So neither does the viewer.

They watch. They nod. They leave.
You taught them for free and waved goodbye.

Give every video ONE next step.
Specific. Low friction.
Said while they're still watching.

Not "visit our site."

posts.thesis: After this post, a marketer will make an honest comparison video, because those viewers are at the deciding moment.
posts.firstComment: The "vs" videos worth making, and where they fit your funnel -> https://sellontube.com/youtube-vs/?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=brand-presence
posts.linkedinPost:
One "us vs them" video booked more demos than a year of our thought-leadership. It had the fewest views on the channel.

On a team I ran content for, we had poured months into big-idea posts. Trends, predictions, the odd hot take. Plenty of likes. The sales team never once traced a deal back to any of it.

Then a rep, half joking, asked us to make an honest "us versus the competitor we kept losing to." We hated it. Too salesy, too direct. We made it anyway. It pulled a fraction of the usual views and booked calls every single week.

Here is why. A comparison viewer already has the problem and already knows the options. They are not learning, they are choosing. It was the only video on our channel that showed up while someone was deciding between us and the other guy.

Wyzowl found 83% of video marketers say video has directly increased sales. This is the video that does it, the one nobody wants to make.

Too salesy? The buyer is already comparing you to them. Better they hear it from you, honestly, than only from your competitor.

Think of the competitor your buyers keep naming. Do you have a single honest video about it?
posts.xPost:
One "us vs them" video booked more demos than a year of our thought leadership.

It had the fewest views on the channel.

A comparison viewer isn't learning.
They're choosing.

The video nobody wants to make is the one that closes.

posts.thesis: After this post, a marketer will make evergreen search videos instead of chasing trends.
posts.linkedinPost:
Chasing trending topics is the fastest way to build a channel nobody searches for next month.

I've watched teams ride a trend to a great week and a dead quarter.

A trend spikes, everyone rushes to post, you catch a week of views. Then the trend dies and takes the video with it. You are left with a library that has an expiry date and a sales team that never felt a thing.

Here is what trends hide: your buyers are not searching what is hot. They are searching what is broken. "How to fix X." "Best tool for Y." "Is Z worth it." Those never trend. They just happen, quietly, every day, forever.

An evergreen video answering one of those keeps getting found for years. A trend video is a fireworks show: bright, loud, gone.

But trends get views. Sure, they do. Rented attention. An evergreen search is an asset you own.

So before you jump on the next hot take, ask a colder question: will anyone search this in 6 months? If not, you are renting attention, not building an asset.

What's one evergreen search your buyers make that you've never made a video for?
posts.xPost:
Chasing trends is the fastest way to build a channel nobody searches for next month.

Your buyers don't search what's hot.
They search what's broken.

"how to fix X"
"best tool for Y"
"is Z worth it"

Those never trend.
They just pay, quietly, for years.

posts.thesis: After this post, a marketer will treat a video as a compounding asset, not a disposable post.
posts.firstComment: The buyer searches worth ranking for -> https://sellontube.com/tools/youtube-seo-tool?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=brand-presence
posts.postAngle: A ranked video keeps converting for years.
posts.linkedinPost:
A LinkedIn post dies in 48 hours. A YouTube video kept booking us demos 18 months later.

On a team I led, we had videos we had forgotten about, made a year and a half earlier, still pulling demo requests every month. Nobody was promoting them. They ranked for a search buyers make, so they kept getting found and kept converting while we slept.

That is the trade almost nobody weighs. Same afternoon, you make a LinkedIn post or a search-optimized video. The post peaks tonight and is gone by Thursday. The video answering "best tool for remote onboarding" can sit near the top of that search for years.

Wyzowl found 82% of marketers say video gives them a good ROI. This is the mechanism behind that number: pay once, earn for years.

Video feels like more work than a post? It is. Once. Then it earns while you sleep, for years.

So stop grading a video by its first week. Grade it by year 2.

Think of your best video from a year ago. Is it still bringing you anything today?
posts.xPost:
A LinkedIn post dies in 48 hours.

A YouTube video kept booking us demos 18 months later.

Same afternoon to make either one.

One peaks tonight and is gone by Thursday.
The other ranks for a buyer search for years.

Stop grading videos by week 1.
Grade them by year 2.
